title: MiCA becomes fully applicable: crypto-asset service provider authorisation and market abuse rules take effect across the EU
summary: Regulation (EU) 2023/1114 (MiCA) reached full application on 30 December 2024. Under Article 149(2), the remaining parts of MiCA -- Title V's authorisation and operating-conditions regime for crypto-asset service providers (CASPs) and Title VI's market-abuse regime for crypto-assets -- became applicable across the EU that day, alongside the asset-referenced and e-money token rules already applicable since 30 June 2024. Authorisation of individual CASPs is carried out by each member state's own national competent authority (NCA), not at EU level; one NCA's authorisation then passports EU-wide. Article 143(3) lets a CASP already lawfully providing services before 30 December 2024 continue until 1 July 2026 or its MiCA decision, whichever is sooner -- a window member states may shorten. ESMA's 17 December 2024 statement confirms these transitional periods vary by member state and apply only to services provided there.
why_it_matters: This is the milestone that turned MiCA from a partly-applicable law into the EU's complete crypto-asset regime: any firm providing exchange, custody, dealing, advisory or similar crypto-asset services in the EU now needs a CASP authorisation from a national regulator, and crypto-asset trading is subject to EU market-abuse rules for the first time. Newcomers should note this radar tracks the EU-level Regulation itself, not the 27 member states' individual CASP licensing decisions, which sit with each national competent authority.

Titles III and IV shall apply from 30 June 2024
